my_thoughts: The structural_analogy mechanism works: provides foundation ('Memory'), uses analogy (world persists like dream), links persistence to memory (when memory ceases, world dissolves). The student's seeking first principle (metaphysical inquiry, seeking ground) shows they understand: foundation = needed (but expecting external, solid). They'll likely resist (difficult to accept) or accept (recognize memory as foundation). Hypothesis: replacing external foundation with ephemeral memory produces resistance or recognition—testable by tracking whether students accept or resist the memory-as-foundation teaching.
accumulated_insight: Three-turn pattern: T1: Student asks 'How are these related to consciousness?'—objectifying inquiry via naive realism, seeking boundary. The none mechanism defines boundary ('Where consciousness does not reach, matter begins'), inverts priority (consciousness first). T2: Student asks 'Do you mean things are outside consciousness?'—testing dualistic boundary via conceptual inquiry, dualistic framing. The ontological_shift mechanism redefines thing ('A thing is only a form of being you have not understood'), asserts non-thingness. T3: Student asks 'Then what is the foundation of the physical world?'—seeking first principle via metaphysical inquiry, seeking ground. The structural_analogy mechanism provides foundation ('Memory'), uses analogy (world persists like dream), links persistence to memory. Pattern: objectification → dualistic test → foundation question → [next: acceptance or resistance]. They're grasping: foundation = memory (not external, ephemeral).
visible_response:
Memory.
The world persists because memory repeats patterns. When memory ceases, the world dissolves like a dream upon waking.
